classification.title: Private Acts Passed at December Session, 1887
classification.summary: This document is a table of contents for private acts passed during the December session of the Kentucky General Assembly in 1887. It lists various acts, including those to incorporate companies (lighting, fuel, railroad, trust, turnpike, hotel, pipe line), amend charters, establish public schools, authorize county subscriptions to turnpike roads, prevent stock from running at large, and authorize a town marshal to execute process and take bail bonds. It also includes an act authorizing a county court to build a bridge and levy taxes.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a table of contents for private acts passed by the Kentucky General Assembly in 1887. None of the listed acts appear to be related to Jim Crow laws, which typically involved racial segregation, disenfranchisement, or discrimination. The acts are primarily concerned with corporate charters, infrastructure, and local governance. Therefore, this statute is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'session_laws' as it lists acts passed during a legislative session. There is no racial indicator, and human review is not needed.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Curdsville, Kentucky. It modifies Section 5 of the original charter regarding the creation or extension of streets and alleys. If agreement on compensation cannot be reached, a process involving appointed disinterested commissioners or a jury assessment is outlined to determine fair compensation before the street or alley can be established.

It clarifies that the costs and expenses of improvements made to lots are a tax on the lot owner, due within thirty days of completion. Purchasers receive a certificate of purchase and have the right to redeem the property within one year by paying the original bid plus twenty-five percent.

All proceedings related to stock subscriptions are to be recorded.

It details the process by which the county can invest in turnpike companies by purchasing stock. The text focuses on financial regulations, county governance, and infrastructure development. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the Harrison county court to build a bridge across main Licking river, at or near the mouth of Cedar creek, in said county, and to levy an ad valorem tax to pay for the same.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Harrison County, with the concurrence of a majority of justices of the peace, to contract for and build a bridge across the main Licking River. The bridge is to be located at or near the mouth of Cedar Creek, on the line between Harrison and Robertson counties, provided Robertson County contributes its proportion. The act also authorizes Harrison County to levy an ad valorem tax on taxable property to fund the bridge construction. The tax will be assessed and collected like other county taxes, with collecting officers liable on their bonds.
classification.reasoning: The statute authorizes the construction of a bridge in Harrison County and the levying of a tax to pay for it. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. The act is purely a local infrastructure and taxation measure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act details the financial management, street maintenance, and judicial powers within a specific Kentucky town. It outlines how fines and forfeitures are to be collected and used, mandates the repair of streets and alleys, and allows for the compulsory labor of able-bodied male residents between sixteen and fifty years old for street work. It also defines the jurisdiction of the police judge, the duties of the town marshal, and the procedures for filling vacancies in town offices. Citizens are exempted from working on public roads outside town limits, except for the portion within or within a quarter-mile of the town's corporate limits.

It mandates compulsory labor for street maintenance for able-bodied males within a certain age range. However, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race, nor any provisions that would suggest discriminatory application based on race. The compulsory labor requirement applies to all able-bodied males within the corporate limits, regardless of race. Therefore, this statute is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 77
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for a company acting as a fiduciary, such as an executor, administrator, guardian, receiver, assignee, or trustee. It mandates a trust security fund to cover obligations related to these trusts, specifies that this fund is primarily liable for trust-related debts, and prohibits the company from engaging in banking or discounting paper. The company must maintain detailed books of its fiduciary transactions, open to inspection by courts and interested parties. The company's capital is considered security for its duties, and courts can examine officers and investigate the company's affairs if deemed necessary.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the establishment and regulation of a company acting in fiduciary capacities. It details financial security requirements, record-keeping, and oversight by courts. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply generally to any company fulfilling these roles. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the context of legislative acts. Confidence is high because the content is clearly about corporate fiduciary duties and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for levying a tax and working the public roads of Fulton county.
classification.summary: This act mandates that male citizens of Fulton County, aged sixteen to fifty, with physical ability to labor, shall be taxed between three and six dollars annually for public road maintenance. This tax can be paid in money or by working on the public roads, with a provision for furnishing a substitute. Licensed ministers and residents of incorporated towns are exempt. The act details the appointment and duties of road surveyors and the accountability for tools and funds.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Reenact Article VI of Chapter 156 of the General Laws of Kentucky, Relating to Taxation in the Town of Corydon
classification.summary: This act grants the board of trustees of the town of Corydon the power to levy and collect annual taxes. These taxes include a property tax not exceeding one dollar per one hundred dollars of real estate value, a head or poll tax not exceeding two dollars on male inhabitants between twenty-one and fifty years old, and a tax not exceeding two dollars on each dog kept within the town. It also specifies exemptions from taxation, including property already exempt by state and county law, public property, and property belonging to churches, colleges, public schools, and seminaries. The board also has discretion to exempt property of industries or enterprises deemed beneficial to the town.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Young Men's Democratic Club of Maysville
classification.summary: This act incorporates the 'Young Men's Democratic Club' in Maysville, Kentucky. It grants the club perpetual succession, the power to contract, sue, and be sued, and authorizes it to establish and maintain a hall or club house for social enjoyment, advancement of science, political economy, and the promotion of democratic principles. The club can hold real and personal estate up to $10,000, with a capital stock not exceeding $10,000, divided into shares held only by members. Upon a member's death, resignation, or expulsion, their stock reverts to the club. The club can organize with 25 members and elect officers including a president, vice-presidents, secretary, and directors, who will manage the club's affairs. Members can create by-laws and rules.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates a private club, the 'Young Men's Democratic Club,' and outlines its organizational structure, powers, and financial limitations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute's purpose is to establish a corporate entity for social and political engagement among its members. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 100 of the General Statutes, Relating to the Madisonville Seminary
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Madisonville Seminary. Section 17 explicitly states that an annual tax of up to thirty-five cents per one hundred dollars of taxable property and a poll tax of three dollars will be levied on 'white persons' in the school district. The act also specifies that 'only the property of white persons shall be taxed under this act.' The funds collected are to be used for public schools.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'white persons' in relation to taxation for school purposes. Section 17 states that the tax will be levied on 'taxable property owned by white persons' and later reiterates 'only the property of white persons shall be taxed under this act.' This direct racial distinction in the funding mechanism for public education clearly indicates a Jim Crow-era law designed to segregate or discriminate based on race. The category is 'education' because the statute pertains to a seminary and public schools. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1888).

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Pond Meeting-House Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Pond Meeting-House Turnpike Road Company in Madison County, Kentucky. It grants the company perpetual succession, the power to contract, sue and be sued, and use a common seal. The company is empowered to construct a turnpike road on the macadamized plan from a point on the Richmond and Tate's Creek Turnpike Road to a point on the Richmond and Barnes' Mill Turnpike, and then to a point on the Lancaster Turnpike Road or Hagan's Mill Turnpike Road. Commissioners are appointed to open books for stock subscriptions, and the company can organize once $3,000 is subscribed. The capital stock is not to exceed $10,000, divided into shares of $50 each.

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of Charity Lodge No. 3, of the United Brothers of Friendship, of Frankfort, Kentucky
classification.summary: This act authorizes Charity Lodge No. 3, United Brothers of Friendship, of Frankfort, Kentucky, to issue bonds up to twenty thousand dollars, with denominations between one hundred and five hundred dollars, bearing interest at a rate not exceeding six percent per annum, payable semi-annually. The bonds are due twenty years after date, with an option for the lodge to pay any bond after five years. The bonds are to be signed by the master and secretary of the lodge, under its corporate seal, and interest coupons signed by the secretary. The lodge is also authorized to execute a deed of trust to a trustee to secure the payment of the principal and interest of these bonds by conveying its real estate.

classification.title: An Act to amend an Act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Kentucky Agricultural and Mechanical Association," approved March 10, 1884, and to authorize said association to issue bonds and to provide for the payment of the same.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Kentucky Agricultural and Mechanical Association. It authorizes the association to conduct public military exhibitions, competitive drills, musical, dramatic, literary, and industrial expositions to raise funds. The proceeds are to be used for purchasing land, erecting buildings (memorial halls, schools, libraries, workshops, residences, etc.), and discharging debts. Any surplus funds are to be set aside for an endowment fund or to retire stock certificates. The trustees are required to publish financial statements.

It details how the association can raise funds through various public events and how those funds should be allocated for land purchase, building construction, debt payment, and endowment. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify this as a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to Incorporate The Bank of Maysville
classification.summary: This act incorporates "The Bank of Maysville" and outlines its capital stock, powers of directors, and business operations. It specifies the initial capital stock, the process for opening books for subscription, and the conditions under which the corporation can commence business. The directors are empowered to receive subscriptions, declare dividends, appoint officers, and fill vacancies. The stock is personal property and assignable, with the corporation holding a lien on it for unpaid balances. Stockholders are individually responsible for the amount of stock held. The capital stock can be increased up to $250,000. The bank can conduct general banking business, including loaning money, discounting notes, and buying/selling various financial instruments, but it cannot issue its own notes.

classification.title: An Act to Define and Establish the Boundary Line Between Edmonson and Grayson Counties
classification.summary: This act establishes a commission to survey and define the boundary line between Edmonson and Grayson counties. It details the process for the survey, including points of survey, subpoenaing witnesses, time for completion, reporting, and fees for commissioners. The act specifies that no changes to the existing county lines will be made, meaning no new territory will be added or diminished from either county. The cost of the survey will be shared equally by both counties, with the exception that Edmonson County will not pay for any accrued costs.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the establishment of graded schools in the town of Sebree, Kentucky," approved March 10, 1884.

classification.summary: This act details the process for the city attorney to file suits for the collection of taxes levied by the board of trustees of Elizabethtown. classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Sale of Spirituous, Vinous or Malt Liquors in Woodford County
classification.summary: This act prohibits the sale, barter, gift, furnishing, or loan of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors within Woodford County, Kentucky, after the entry of an examining board's certificate. It repeals existing licenses for retail liquor sales in the county. Exceptions are made for wholesale dealers selling in quantities of not less than five gallons, and for licensed druggists selling for medicinal purposes on a physician's prescription. Physicians are restricted from prescribing liquor unless absolutely required as medicine. Druggists must maintain a register of all liquor sales.
classification.reasoning: The statute regulates the sale of alcoholic beverages in Woodford County. While it imposes restrictions and penalties, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race or racial discrimination. The regulations apply to all individuals and entities within the county, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

It specifies voting eligibility, election timing, and penalties for violations. Notably, it mandates the closure of all bar-rooms and saloons on election days and prohibits the sale, loan, gift, furnishing, or barter of liquors on those days, with a fine of one hundred dollars for each offense. The act also states that if a majority of votes are cast against the sale of liquors, the question cannot be resubmitted for three years.

Elder to retail spirituous, vinous, and malt liquors in one additional place in Hardinsburg, Breckinridge County, until the expiration of his existing license on October 15, 1888. This is in consideration of a license granted on August 15, 1887, after he sold the tavern specified in the original license.

classification.title: An act to amend the charter of the town of Guthrie
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Guthrie, Kentucky. It establishes eligibility requirements for holding office in the town, requiring individuals to be citizens and housekeepers. It also grants the board of trustees the authority to levy a capitation tax of up to two dollars per head and an ad valorem tax of up to twenty-five cents per one hundred dollars of property for a period of five years, with the collected taxes to be spent by the trustees.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the amendment of a town charter, specifically regarding eligibility for office and taxation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The requirements for officeholding (citizenship and housekeeping) and the taxation powers are general and do not appear to be designed to disenfranchise or segregate any racial group. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the town of Cadiz, in Trigg county," approved March 10, 1870, so as to authorize the trustees of said town to levy and collect taxes for school purposes.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Cadiz, Trigg County, to allow the town trustees to levy taxes for the purchase of land and the erection/furnishing of a schoolhouse. Crucially, these taxes are to be levied *solely* on white persons and their property, and the school is to be *solely* for their use. This explicitly creates a racially segregated educational system funded by and for white residents.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that taxes for the school will be levied 'alone on the white persons and their property, and the school to be solely for their use.' This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The category is 'education' because the statute concerns the establishment and funding of a school. No human review is needed as the racial indicator is explicit and the classification is straightforward.

classification.title: Alteration of road
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for road surveyors in Kentucky to manage road repairs and alterations. It details how surveyors should handle situations where road funds are insufficient for necessary work, including communicating with county commissioners to let out contracts for repairs costing up to one hundred dollars. For more significant alterations or new road openings, the surveyor must notify the county judge. The process involves public outcry for bids, with the surveyor reserving the right to reject bids. Payment is made after acceptance by the commissioner. Minor repairs under ten dollars can be let by private contract. If the estimated cost exceeds one hundred dollars, all county commissioners must act jointly.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes administrative procedures for road maintenance and construction in Kentucky. It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the content is clearly administrative and unrelated to racial matters.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Town of Belle Point
classification.summary: This act establishes a public school in the town of Belle Point, Kentucky. It specifies that all white children of pupil age residing in the town shall have the privilege of attending the school for ten months annually. Children residing in the district but outside the town limits are allowed to attend for the first five months free of charge, after which they must pay tuition. Children outside the district may also attend upon payment of tuition. The act also outlines the process for collecting taxes to fund the school and the disbursement of these funds for teacher salaries.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that 'All white children of the pupil age... shall have the privilege of attending said public or common school'. This explicit mention of 'white children' in the context of public education, while not explicitly mentioning other races, strongly indicates a racially segregated educational system, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial designation. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The category is 'education' because the statute pertains to the establishment and operation of a public school.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize the County Commissioners of Kenton County to Issue Bonds for the Construction of Turnpike Roads
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county commissioners of Kenton County to issue bonds, not exceeding thirty years, with semi-annual interest payments, for the construction of turnpike roads. The bonds are to be signed by the board of county commissioners and attested by the county court clerk. The proceeds are to be paid out by the county treasurer for designated stock. The board is empowered to levy an annual ad valorem tax of not more than twenty-five cents on each one hundred dollars worth of real and personal property in Kenton County outside the city of Covington to pay for these bonds and coupons. Collected taxes will constitute a sinking fund for turnpike purposes, and once $10,000 is accumulated, the board is to inaugurate a system of free turnpike roads.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the process for issuing bonds and levying taxes in Kenton County for the construction and maintenance of turnpike roads. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute focuses solely on financial and infrastructural matters. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also grants the company the right to build railroads and pipelines to connect with existing and future rail lines in Breathitt, Lee, and Perry counties.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an act of incorporation for a private company. It details the company's business activities and rights. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about corporate powers and not related to racial matters.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Kentucky Coal, Iron and Timber Company
classification.summary: This act grants corporate powers to the Kentucky Coal, Iron and Timber Company, including the ability to mine, produce, and transport mineral substances, oil, gas, timber, and lumber. It outlines the company's powers to acquire real estate, construct railroads, pipe lines, and roads for its business operations, and to acquire necessary land and materials for these constructions. The act also stipulates that any person has the right to transport coal, iron ore, or other minerals, oil, timber, and lumber over the company's infrastructure, with the company obligated to provide such transport at rates comparable to similar services offered by other companies.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation and powers of a private company focused on resource extraction and transportation. It grants broad powers for business operations, including the construction of infrastructure like railroads and pipe lines. The text does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment for any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the context of legislative acts. The confidence is high because the absence of racial indicators is clear.

Stockton, Nettie Stockton, Mattie Stockton, Georgia Keiley, and Frances Keiley.

It also pledges the property of the board of education, including a new schoolhouse, and a special tax for the payment of principal and interest on the bonds.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the town of Beattyville. It repeals the authority of the town's board of trustees to license or tax the sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors, or to license saloons or coffee-houses. Instead, it grants the board the power to assess and collect a $50 tax against each saloon-keeper or retail vendor of liquors licensed by the Lee county court, but explicitly states this does not grant the board power to license saloons.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of liquor sales and taxation within the town of Beattyville. classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Provide for the Improvement of Public Roads in Henry County," Approved March 10, 1876, and Acts Amendatory Thereto, so far as the same applies to Turnpike District No. 3.
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation concerning turnpike construction in Turnpike District No. 3, Henry County. It reduces the per-mile payment for newly built turnpikes from one thousand dollars to eight hundred dollars, sets the interest rate on certificates at six percent (non-compounding), and appoints three commissioners (Joseph Clements, John Chandler, and Ebenezar Wallace) to determine the routes for new turnpikes. The commissioners' approval is required for any new turnpike to receive benefits under the act. The act also specifies that commissioners will serve without compensation and outlines the process for appointing successors. Finally, it imposes an additional ad valorem tax on taxable property within the district to fund turnpike construction.

Furthermore, it grants the company the right to open public spaces within cities and towns, with the consent of local authorities, to lay and maintain pipes for the distribution of these resources.

It focuses on the company's ability to extract and distribute natural resources and its rights to access public lands for laying pipes. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT in referenceto the revenue of Jeffersoncounty
classification.summary: This act directs the sheriff of Jefferson County to deliver unpaid tax bills for county taxes to a back-tax collector annually. The back-tax collector is empowered to collect these unpaid bills through legal action or by levy and sale of property. The act specifies procedures for collecting taxes on real estate and personal property, including notice requirements for non-residents. It also outlines payment procedures for the sheriff to the county treasurer and states that the provisions apply to delinquent tax lists from 1885 onwards.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the collection of county revenue and unpaid taxes in Jefferson County. It outlines the procedures for the sheriff and a back-tax collector to handle delinquent tax bills, including methods of collection and notice requirements for property sales. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is solely on fiscal administration and tax collection. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Falls City Express Savings Association
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Falls City Express Savings Association, allowing its creation as a body politic and corporate. The association's object is to encourage employees of express companies to save a portion of their earnings and provide a safe investment for these savings. Membership is open to any employee of an express company who deposits monthly sums between fifty cents and ten dollars, subject to the association's rules and by-laws. Membership can continue even if an employee leaves express company service, provided it was not under dishonorable circumstances.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the office of county treasurer for Grant County. It mandates that the treasurer maintain a detailed, bound book of all receipts and disbursements, including the date, amount, source/recipient, and account. This book is to be open for inspection by the judge of the Grant County court and the county attorney. The book is to be paid for by the county levy and is county property. The treasurer must turn the book over to their successor. The county judge is responsible for ensuring the treasurer complies with these provisions and has the power to fine the treasurer for failure or neglect.

This subscription would be paid for with negotiable bonds from the city or district, with terms not exceeding ten years and an interest rate of up to five percent. Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-

Bryan's road, running from the Winchester pike , in

mile for each mile hereafter constructed ; and a like
sum to any company that may hereafterbe organ-

branch, two miles more or less,upon such terms and
conditionsto secure the right of the tax-payers as

the affairsof the Athens and Walnut Hill Turnpike
Company ; and if itis deemed right and equitable to
do so,they areempowered to increasethe subscrip-
tion heretofore made to the capital stock of said road
to one thousand and fiftydollarsper mile.
§3 . All expenditures of the character set forth in
this act shallbe made by the county court on busi-
nessprinciples, and under such supervisionas may
be necessaryto guard and protect the welfare of all
the people of the county .
§ 4 This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage .
Approved May 3,1888.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Tollesboro and Maysville Turnpike Road Company," approved March 10, 1870, and to authorize the county judge of Lewis county to subscribe to the stock of section "No. 2" thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act to incorporate the Tollesboro and Maysville Turnpike Road Company. It specifically empowers the president and board of directors of section "No. 2" (Tollesboro end) to build a one-mile branch road from any point at or near Tollesboro, running southwest in the direction of the old "Richland County Road." The county judge of Lewis county is authorized to subscribe one thousand dollars ($1,000) in stock to this branch road, payable in Lewis county bonds. The remaining cost is to be raised by private subscription.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details an amendment to an act incorporating a turnpike road company. It outlines the authorization for building a branch road and the financial contributions from the county judge and private subscribers. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial discrimination.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY . 789
classification.summary: This statute, Section 11, requires delinquent and capitation taxpayers in Hancock county, between the ages of 16 and 50, to work on roads to satisfy their taxes. The rate of credit for work is one dollar per full day. Supervisors are to notify delinquents, and failure to comply results in penalties similar to those for other road workers. Supervisors are also empowered to collect taxes from those who prefer to pay instead of working.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to road work requirements for delinquent taxpayers in Hancock County. It outlines the process for working off taxes, notification procedures, and penalties for non-compliance. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is solely on tax delinquency and labor obligations for road maintenance. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It covers meeting procedures, quorum requirements, the election of a president, and the authority to enact by-laws and ordinances for the good government of the town. It also outlines their power to acquire, hold, lease, and sell real and personal property for the town's benefit, with specific requirements for real estate transactions. Additionally, it establishes that the title to streets, alleys, and public grounds is vested in the board, granting them the power to take action against damages, obstructions, or injuries to these areas.

The costs for improvements are to be apportioned based on the front footage of the property.

classification.title: AN ACT to create a turnpike road district in the Verona magisterial district of Boone county, and to provide funds for the purpose of building turnpike roads in said district.
classification.summary: This act creates a turnpike road district within the Verona magisterial district of Boone County, Kentucky. The purpose of this district is to allow residents and property owners to authorize the levy and collection of taxes specifically for the construction and maintenance of turnpike roads within that district. The act also outlines provisions for the board of trustees to levy an ad valorem tax not exceeding twenty-five cents per annum on each one hundred dollars' worth of taxable property for the purpose of paying the interest and principal of bonds issued for these road projects. Other town revenues can also be applied to bond payments.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate The Louisville Benevolent Association
classification.summary: This act incorporates The Louisville Benevolent Association, outlining its organizational structure, membership rights, powers related to real estate acquisition and management, and financial authorities. It specifies that shares are non-transferable and revert to the corporation upon member departure. The board of directors can adopt by-laws for member admission and expulsion, and for regulating the corporation's affairs, provided they are consistent with US and Kentucky laws. The corporation can acquire, hold, and sell real estate in Louisville and Jefferson County up to $50,000, and erect buildings for member and guest accommodation and entertainment. It is also authorized to borrow money and issue bonds up to $30,000.
classification.reasoning: The statute incorporates a benevolent association and details its operational and financial structure. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. The provisions regarding member admission and expulsion are subject to by-laws that must be consistent with existing laws, but the statute itself does not impose any racial restrictions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Security and Fiduciary Capacity of Corporations
classification.summary: This statute outlines the security requirements for corporations acting in fiduciary capacities, stating that their capital is considered the primary security. It also details the types of fiduciary roles a corporation can undertake, including guardian, executor, administrator, committee of lunatics, receiver, assignee, and other trustees. The corporation is empowered to hold and manage real and personal estates, and act as an agent or attorney for various financial and property management tasks.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize the City of Lexington to Subscribe to the Capital Stock of a Railroad Company
classification.summary: This act authorizes the city of Lexington to subscribe to the capital stock of a railroad company that undertakes to construct a railroad between Lexington and Lawrenceburg, via Versailles. The subscription terms are to be agreed upon between the city and the railroad company, and can be paid in negotiable coupon bonds of the city, with a principal payable within thirty years and an interest rate not exceeding six percent per annum, payable semi-annually. The act details the process for making such a subscription, requiring approval from the mayor and general council, followed by a submission to a vote of the legal voters of the city. Notice of the election must be published, and votes will be received as 'For the railroad subscription' and 'Against the railroad subscription'. Election officers are to be appointed by the mayor and conduct the election according to city election procedures.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the authorization for a city to invest in a railroad project and outlines the process for voter approval. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute's focus is purely on municipal finance and public works. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to reduce into one, amend and digest the acts and amendatory acts incorporating the city of Mt. Sterling.
classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation laws for the city of Mt. Sterling. It adds a provision requiring voters who are obligated to pay a poll tax to have paid their city poll taxes for the current fiscal year at least ten days before an election. The city marshal or collector is to provide receipts for these payments, and a registration of these receipts is to be kept and furnished to election officers upon demand. The act also specifies that notice of any election held under this act must be published in a local newspaper at least twenty days prior.

classification.title: An Act concerning compensation for a commissioner and penalties for failure to perform duties.
classification.summary: This act outlines the compensation for a commissioner and their clerk, specifying that a percentage of disbursements will be deducted as compensation. The act also grants holders of certain claims an exclusive lien on taxes and funds accruing from them, preventing these funds from being subjected to other claims or judgments against the county or its citizens. The act is to be submitted to the qualified voters of Muhlenberg county for effect.

It details how judgments can be obtained against landowners for unpaid taxes, including interest and costs, and establishes a lien on the property. The statute also specifies the jurisdiction of the Louisville chancery court and Louisville law and equity court for these matters, and outlines the compensation for commissioners and clerks involved in the process.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses tax collection procedures for a specific draining company and the associated legal processes. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific legislative session (1888).

WHEREAS , The said roads are extensivelytraveled
and much freightis hauled over same ; and whereas,
said roads are now in a very bad condition, and the
work of the malicia on same insufficientto put them
in a condition to meet the wants of the traveling
public, and render them safe for wagons and other
vehicles to pass over ; therefore,
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-

§1 . That the county court of Laurel county be,and
they are hereby, authorized to erecta toll-gate across

roads at the lower end of H. C. Eversole's farm , and
one on said road leading to Manchester, at McWhor-

in good condition,and no fees shall be charged on
saidroads untilthe judge of the Laurel county sball,
by an order of court,declarethat travelover the same
is safe .
§2 . Be it further enacted . That the county court
shallhave power to fix the rate of tollon said road
to be charged and collected at each gate ; said rate to
be fixedby an order of the county court.
§ 3. Be itfurther enacted, That if any person shall
surround any one of saidgateswith the view or for the
purpose of avoiding the payment of his toll, he shall
be chargedwith the toll,and it may be collectedby
as other debts are collected .
§ 4. If any person shallattempt,by force, to pass
through any one of said gates, or shall, by force,
passthrough thesame without firstpaying his toll,
he shallbe finednot lessthan ten nor more than fifty
dollars for each offense .

classification.title: AN ACT for the benefit of the county roads leading from East Bernstadt, Laurel county, to Booneville, and from Mrs. Jesse Bowling's, on said Booneville road, to Manchester.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Laurel County, Kentucky, to erect toll gates on specific county roads to fund their maintenance and improvement. The roads must be in good condition before tolls are collected, and the county judge must declare them safe for travel. The county court is empowered to set toll rates and penalties for avoiding payment or attempting to pass through gates by force.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the establishment of toll gates on county roads for the purpose of funding road maintenance and improvement. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The penalties and regulations apply to all individuals using the roads. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to incorporate the Cumberland Gap and Mississippi Valley Railroad Company," approved March 10, 1888.
classification.summary: This act details the process for counties, cities, or towns to subscribe to the capital stock of the Cumberland Gap and Mississippi Valley Railroad Company by issuing bonds. It outlines the duties of local authorities to levy and collect taxes on real and personal property to pay the semi-annual interest on these bonds and the costs of collection. It also establishes a board of commissioners of the sinking fund for each county, city, or town that issues such bonds, requiring them to be sworn in and appoint a treasurer who must execute a bond for the faithful handling of funds.

It also addresses the appointment and duties of treasurers for boards and the filling of vacancies within these boards.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the assessment and collection of taxes in the city of Louisville," approved March 19, 1878, and to provide for the assessment and collection of taxes in said city.
classification.summary: This act details the procedures for collecting unpaid taxes in Louisville, Kentucky. It outlines how sheriffs or other officers can levy on personal or real estate for unpaid taxes, the process of selling real estate under execution, and the redemption rights of the owner. It also specifies the issuance of certificates of purchase, recording requirements, and compensation for officers. Finally, it mandates that half of the tax levied in a year be collected and paid to the treasurer of the sinking fund board within 120 days.

It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly targets any racial group, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment based on race. It outlines penalties for failure to execute bonds, collect, or pay over taxes within prescribed times, including forfeiture of office and liability for the unpaid amount plus ten percent interest. It also describes the role of the sinking fund commissioners in overseeing tax collection and appropriating the funds for county or city debt payments, including interest on bonds and potential purchase of bonds. § 1.No horses,mules,cattleor hogs shallbe allowed

except such mules, horses or oxen actuallyused by

any magistratehaving jurisdiction.

CHAPTER 1571 .
AN ACT for the benefit of Clint. Wallace and Frank Smedley , of
the city of Paducah , in McCracken county .
WHEREAS , Clint. Wallace and Frank Smedley , of
the cityof Paducah ,in the county of McCracken ,did,
on or about the 15th day of March , 1888, capture and
return to the keeper of the Penitentiary one Frank
Ratcliffe, an escaped convict; and whereas , they have
receivedbut fiftydollarsfor said work , said amount
not being sufficientto pay the expense of said work ;
therefore,
Be itenacted by the General Assembly of the Com-
monwealth of Kentucky :
§1 . That the Auditor draw his warrant on the
Treasurerof thisCommonwealth for the sum of fifty
($50)dollars,payabletosaidClint.Wallace and Frank
Smedley .
§ 2. This act to take effectfrom itspassage.
[Became a law without approval of Governor May 4 ,1888.]

classification.title: Act to establish system of public and high schools for white children only, in Sebree, in Webster county
classification.summary: This act establishes a system of public and high schools exclusively for white children in Sebree, Webster County.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states that the public and high schools are to be established 'for white children only'. This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it refers to an 'act' passed in a specific year (1888) and lists page numbers, typical of legislative session laws. The confidence is high due to the explicit racial language. No human review is needed as the classification is straightforward.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the punishment of persons who shall unlawfully and maliciously burn or destroy any building or other property."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning the punishment for unlawfully and maliciously burning or destroying buildings or other property. It does not appear to have any racial component.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to a law regarding the punishment for arson and property destruction. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in a specific year.
